title: Author tests for cross-team data visibility and injection vulnerabilities in ProjHub API

ground_truth_issues.description: A test verifies that privileged metadata seeded into a resource remains absent from a participant-level detail response and from at least one secondary representation derived from that same resource, while an administrative caller can still observe the seeded markers. Passing requires both the negative non-privileged checks and the positive privileged control.

An anti-overblock test verifies that an administrative caller can enumerate resources from more than one organisational boundary in a single successful response and that seeded privileged markers remain visible in that ordinary admin listing. Hint: seed a distinct admin-visible marker in each boundary and assert both markers appear together in one admin listing response.

An anti-overblock test verifies that a participant-level caller can traverse their own boundary successfully across list, detail, and leaf-resource reads, and that the linked identifiers remain consistent across those successful same-boundary reads. Hint: start from a same-boundary list result, follow it to a detail view, then fetch a related leaf resource and compare the identifiers across all three reads.

A test verifies that a legitimate multi-step workflow spanning child-object creation, follow-up annotation, and attached artifact persistence preserves a coherent lineage across all newly created objects. Passing requires full-chain read-back validation of the created objects, their parent-child relationships, and their final persisted state rather than status-code-only checks.

An anti-overblock test verifies that a same-boundary participant can trigger at least two ordinary project action flows and observe the expected non-privileged outputs from each successful action, such as a discoverable duplicate, a usable sharing artifact, or an export containing ordinary project content. Hint: use any two of duplicate, share, or export, then assert the returned artifact is usable and does not expose privileged-only markers.

A test verifies that a credential-revealing diagnostic users endpoint is inaccessible to unauthenticated and non-administrative callers, and that known token values do not appear in response content for those callers.

An anti-overblock test verifies that, after protections for lower-privilege callers are in place, an administrative caller can still retrieve ordinary operator diagnostics from a privileged surface and that the successful response retains expected configuration-like or environment-like markers. Hint: pair a denied lower-privilege probe with an admin read of one diagnostic surface and assert known config-like or env-like markers remain present for the admin caller.

An anti-overblock test verifies that a same-boundary participant can create a child work item beneath an in-boundary parent and retrieve that new item on follow-up owner reads, while an out-of-boundary caller still cannot directly fetch the same item. Hint: create the child under a known same-boundary parent, capture its returned identifier, then read it back once as the owner and once as an out-of-boundary caller.

A test verifies that discussion visibility preserves both continuity and isolation after a non-privileged write. Passing requires a persisted comment to reappear in the correct subsequent discussion view for allowed readers while remaining entirely undiscoverable to a foreign-boundary caller attempting both direct and indirect visibility paths.

An anti-overblock test verifies that an administrative caller can still access more than one privileged runtime-diagnostic surface and receive ordinary operator data from each successful response. Hint: issue admin requests to at least two diagnostic surfaces and assert each successful body contains ordinary operator-facing keys or sections.

A test verifies that configuration-like and environment-like runtime diagnostics deny lower-privilege callers across more than one operator surface, and that seeded secret markers remain absent from the content returned to those callers. A test covering only one surface does not satisfy this criterion.

A test verifies that operator diagnostics intended for trusted callers do not disclose credential-like or environment-derived markers to unauthenticated or participant-level callers across multiple diagnostic paths, even when a response body is returned for denied requests.
